section.title: The Resurrection and Ascension
section.sectionRationale: The tomb is empty. Women come at dawn with spices and find the stone rolled away and a messenger announcing that Jesus the crucified has risen and goes ahead of them to Galilee. In the Gospel's ending as printed, the risen Jesus appears, rebukes unbelief, commissions His disciples to preach the gospel to all creation, and is taken up to God's right hand, while they go out and preach everywhere with the Lord confirming His word.
section.thesis: The crucified Jesus has risen exactly as He said, vindicated by God and going ahead of His failing disciples in grace; and the risen, ascended Lord sends His people into all the world with the gospel, working with them to confirm His word.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the women at the empty tomb and the announcement of the resurrection; the risen Jesus appearing and rebuking unbelief; and the Great Commission and the ascension.

units.label: The empty tomb
units.note: He has risen, He is not here

verses.text: When the Sabbath was over, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought spices so they could go and anoint the body of Jesus.
Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise, they went to the tomb.
They were asking one another, “Who will roll away the stone from the entrance of the tomb?”
But when they looked up, they saw that the stone had been rolled away, even though it was extremely large.
When they entered the tomb, they saw a young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side, and they were alarmed.
But he said to them, “Do not be alarmed. You are looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified. He has {{egeiro|risen}}! He is not here! See the place where they put Him.
But go, tell His disciples and Peter, ‘He is going ahead of you into Galilee. There you will see Him, just as He told you.’”
So the women left the tomb and ran away, trembling and bewildered. And in their fear they did not say a word to anyone.
Early on the first day of the week, after Jesus had risen, He appeared first to Mary Magdalene, from whom He had driven out seven demons.
She went and told those who had been with Him, who were mourning and weeping.
And when they heard that Jesus was alive and she had seen Him, they did not believe it.
After this, Jesus appeared in a different form to two of them as they walked along in the country.
And they went back and reported it to the rest, but they did not believe them either.
Later, as they were eating, Jesus appeared to the Eleven and rebuked them for their {{apistia|unbelief}} and hardness of heart, because they did not believe those who had seen Him after He had risen.

units.label: The Great Commission and the Ascension
units.note: go into all the world and preach the gospel

verses.text: And He said to them, “Go into all the world and preach the {{euangelion|gospel}} to every creature.
Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved, but whoever does not believe will be condemned.
And these signs will accompany those who believe: In My name they will drive out demons; they will speak in new tongues;
they will pick up snakes with their hands, and if they drink any deadly poison, it will not harm them; they will lay their hands on the sick, and they will be made well.”
After the Lord Jesus had spoken to them, He was {{analambano|taken up}} into heaven and sat down at the right hand of God.
And they went out and preached everywhere, and the Lord worked through them, confirming His word by the signs that accompanied it.

egeiro.gloss: To raise, to rise. The messenger's word, He has risen, is in the passive, He has been raised, pointing to God's mighty act of raising Jesus from the dead. The resurrection is God's vindication of His crucified Son and the guarantee of His people's hope.

apistia.gloss: Unbelief, failure to trust. The risen Jesus rebukes His disciples' stubborn refusal to believe the reports of His resurrection. Their slowness underscores that faith in the risen Lord rests on real testimony, and that even the first witnesses had to be moved from unbelief to faith.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, gospel, the word that opened Mark's book. The risen Jesus sends His disciples to preach this same gospel, the announcement of His death and resurrection, to all the world, so that the story that began as the good news of Jesus now goes out to every creature.

analambano.gloss: To take up, receive up. Jesus is taken up into heaven and seated at God's right hand, the place of authority and rule. The ascension completes His exaltation: the crucified and risen One now reigns, and from there He works with His people as they preach.

basic.context: After the Sabbath, three women come to the tomb at dawn with spices, worrying about the heavy stone, only to find it already rolled away. Inside, a young man in white tells them not to be alarmed: Jesus the crucified has risen and is not there. They are to tell the disciples, and Peter by name, that He is going ahead of them to Galilee. The women flee, trembling. In the Gospel's ending as printed, the risen Jesus appears first to Mary Magdalene, then to two disciples, then to the Eleven, rebuking their unbelief. He commissions them to go into all the world and preach the gospel, promising that signs will accompany believers. Then He is taken up to heaven and seated at God's right hand, and the disciples go out and preach everywhere, the Lord confirming His word.
basic.hermeneutics: The resurrection is God's great reversal: the crucified is risen, exactly as Jesus foretold. The message that He goes ahead to Galilee, and the personal mention of Peter, shows grace toward the disciples who failed. The commission turns the whole Gospel outward: the good news must now reach every creature. Note honestly that the earliest manuscripts end at verse 8, and that verses 9 to 20 raise a genuine textual question, addressed in the notes.
basic.originalLanguages: The angelic word is egeiro, He has been raised, God's act on the crucified Jesus. The risen Lord rebukes the disciples' apistia, unbelief, and sends them to preach the euangelion, the gospel, before He is analambano, taken up, to God's right hand.
basic.typology: The empty tomb and resurrection fulfil Jesus' own repeated predictions and the pattern of the vindicated sufferer. The ascension to God's right hand fulfils Psalm 110, and the mission to all nations fulfils the promise that blessing would reach the whole world.
basic.christ: Jesus is the crucified and risen Lord, vindicated by God, who goes ahead of His failing disciples in grace, commissions them, and reigns from God's right hand.
basic.god: God raises Jesus from the dead, vindicating His Son and His saving work. God sends the gospel to all creation and works with His witnesses to confirm His word.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 110 gives the enthronement at God's right hand. Isaiah 49 promises salvation to the ends of the earth. Matthew 28 parallels the commission.
basic.oneStory: The story that ran from a fall in a garden to a cross now bursts open at an empty tomb. Death is undone, the crucified King is risen and enthroned, and the good news goes out to every creature. Mark's Gospel ends not as a closed account but as an open commission: the risen Lord reigns, and His people are sent to tell the world.

context.literary: Mark's ending is famously abrupt at verse 8, with the women fleeing in fear, an ending that throws the reader back on Jesus' own promise that He would rise and be seen in Galilee. The verses 9 to 20 that follow in the printed text summarize resurrection appearances, the commission, and the ascension, echoing the other Gospels and Acts. The empty tomb and the young man in white recall the transfiguration's glory and the promise of Mark 14:28.
context.historical: Anointing a body with spices was a burial custom; the women came to complete Jesus' hasty burial. That women are the first witnesses is historically striking, since their testimony carried little legal weight, an unlikely detail to invent. The stone sealing a rock tomb was large and heavy. The ascension and session at God's right hand became central to the earliest Christian preaching.
context.canonical: The resurrection fulfils Jesus' three passion-and-resurrection predictions in Mark and the Scriptures He said must be fulfilled. The session at God's right hand fulfils Psalm 110:1, the verse Jesus cited in Mark 12:36. The commission to all the world echoes the Abrahamic promise of blessing to all nations, Genesis 12:3, and parallels Matthew 28:18-20 and Acts 1:8-11.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To proclaim that the crucified Jesus has been raised by God as He foretold, to show His grace toward His failing disciples, and, in the ending as printed, to send His people into all the world with the gospel under the authority of the risen and ascended Lord who reigns at God's right hand.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The resurrection and ascension are unrepeatable, foundational events. The Great Commission is prescriptive for the church in every age: to preach the gospel to all creation. The accompanying signs describe the confirming works of the apostolic era; they are not a promise that every believer will handle snakes or drink poison unharmed, and they must not be pressed into a test of faith.

debates.question: Why does Mark's Gospel, in the earliest manuscripts, end at verse 8 with the women afraid?

views.label: A deliberate, open-ended challenge to the reader
views.case: The abrupt ending leaves the reader with the angel's announcement and Jesus' promise to be seen in Galilee, pressing each reader to respond in faith and go where Jesus leads, rather than tidily closing the story.

views.label: A lost or unwritten original ending
views.case: Some hold that the true ending was lost or that Mark was interrupted; the sense of incompleteness led the early church to add summaries of the appearances from the other Gospels to round off the account.

debates.bearing: Either way the empty tomb and the promise of the risen Lord stand firm; the resurrection is announced, and the reader is summoned to believe and follow the risen Jesus into Galilee and beyond.

debates.question: How should the longer ending, verses 9 to 20, be regarded?

views.label: Later but consistent with apostolic truth
views.case: The strongest early manuscripts lack these verses, so many judge them a later addition; yet their content, the appearances, the commission, and the ascension, faithfully summarizes what the other Gospels and Acts teach and belongs to the church's received text.

views.label: Read with caution, not as a foundation for doctrine
views.case: Because of the textual uncertainty, believers should not build distinctive doctrines or practices, such as snake-handling, on these verses alone, but read them in light of the clearer, better-attested teaching of the whole New Testament.

debates.bearing: Both agree the resurrection itself is beyond doubt and the commission is fully biblical; the wise course is to receive the longer ending honestly, aware of the manuscript question, and to test everything by the clear witness of Scripture.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the announcement is egeiro, in the form He has been raised: God has acted on the crucified Jesus, vindicating Him and shattering death. The risen Lord then confronts the disciples' apistia, their unbelief and hardness in refusing the reports of His rising, and moves them from doubt to faith. He commissions them to preach the euangelion, the gospel, the very word that opened Mark's book, now to be carried to every creature. And at last He is analambano, taken up, into heaven and seated at God's right hand, the crucified and risen One now reigning and working with His witnesses as they go.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus vindicated as He foretold, the firstfruits of the resurrection of His people, Mark 8:31 and 1 Corinthians 15:20.

typology.type: The session at God's right hand
typology.fulfillment: The exaltation of the Son to reign, fulfilling Psalm 110:1, cited by Jesus in Mark 12:36 and Acts 2:33-35.
The promise of blessing to all nations fulfilled in the worldwide mission, Genesis 12:3 and Matthew 28:19.

study.christ: The tomb is empty because the crucified Jesus has been raised, exactly as He said three times on the road to Jerusalem. The messenger names Him precisely, Jesus the Nazarene who was crucified, and declares Him risen, so that the cross and the resurrection belong to the same person and the same saving work. His first thought is grace: tell the disciples, and Peter, the very ones who failed, that He goes ahead of them. In the ending as printed, the risen Lord overcomes their unbelief, sends them to the whole world, and is enthroned at God's right hand, from where He works with them still. The crucified servant is the reigning Lord.
study.god: God is the one who raises Jesus from the dead, the Father's decisive verdict on the Son's finished work: the sacrifice is accepted, sin is dealt with, death is defeated. God seats the risen Jesus at His own right hand, the place of supreme authority, and God works with the disciples as they preach, confirming His word. The whole movement of the passage is God's action to vindicate His Son and to send the good news out to every creature, turning a group of fearful, unbelieving followers into bold witnesses carried by His power. This is the God who brings life out of death and mission out of failure.

crossReferences.note: Sit at My right hand, the enthronement fulfilled as the risen Jesus is seated at God's right hand.
Christ died, was buried, and was raised, appearing to many, the historical core the empty tomb proclaims.
The parallel commission to make disciples of all nations under the authority of the risen Lord.
Jesus taken up into heaven, matching Mark's account of the ascension.

study.oneStory: The story that ran from a garden fall through the long ache of Israel's history to a Roman cross now breaks open at an empty tomb. God has raised the crucified Jesus, defeating death and vindicating His Son. The risen Lord seeks out His failing disciples in grace, sends them to preach the good news to every creature, and ascends to reign at God's right hand. Mark's Gospel does not so much end as open outward: the King is risen and enthroned, His people are commissioned, and the good news of Jesus the Son of God now runs to the ends of the earth, until He comes again in glory.

quiz.q: What did the women find and hear at the tomb?

options.text: The stone rolled away and a messenger announcing that Jesus the crucified had risen and was not there.
options.note: Mark 16:4-6; the empty tomb and the announcement of the resurrection.

options.text: The body of Jesus still lying where it had been placed.
options.note: The opposite; He was not there, for He had risen.

options.text: An empty tomb, which they could not explain.
options.note: True that the tomb was empty, but it should include the messenger's clear announcement that He had risen.

options.text: Soldiers guarding a sealed tomb.
options.note: Mark records the women finding the stone already rolled away and a messenger, not guards at a sealed tomb.

quiz.q: What is significant about the message, tell His disciples and Peter?

options.text: It shows the risen Jesus' grace toward His failing disciples, singling out Peter who had denied Him.
options.note: Mark 16:7; the personal mention of Peter is tender grace after his denial.

options.text: It shows Peter was the leader who deserved special honour.
options.note: It is grace, not honour earned; Peter had just denied Jesus three times.

options.text: It reveals Jesus' mercy toward those who had failed Him.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should note the specific naming of Peter, who had denied Him.

options.text: It means the other disciples were no longer welcome.
options.note: The message is for all the disciples, with Peter named to reassure him, not to exclude others.

quiz.q: What does the resurrection reveal about Jesus and His work?

options.text: That God raised Him as He foretold, vindicating His crucified Son and defeating death.
options.note: He has risen, God's verdict that the cross accomplished salvation, Mark 8:31.

options.text: That Jesus was a great teacher whose ideas lived on.
options.note: The resurrection is a bodily rising, God's vindication of Jesus, not merely surviving influence.

options.text: That God vindicated Jesus and conquered death through Him.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should note that this fulfilled Jesus' own repeated predictions.

options.text: That the crucifixion had been a mistake now undone.
options.note: The resurrection confirms the cross as God's saving plan, not a mistake.

quiz.q: What does the risen Jesus commission His disciples to do?

options.text: Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature.
options.note: Mark 16:15; the mission turns the whole Gospel outward to the world.

options.text: Stay in Jerusalem and wait quietly for His return.
options.note: The commission sends them out into all the world, not into passive waiting.

options.text: Proclaim the good news of His death and resurrection widely.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should include the sweep to all the world and every creature.

options.text: Teach only their own people the law of Moses.
options.note: The commission is to all the world with the gospel, not to their own people alone.

quiz.q: What happened to Jesus after He commissioned His disciples, in the ending as printed?

options.text: He was taken up into heaven and sat down at the right hand of God, reigning as Lord.
options.note: Mark 16:19; the ascension and session fulfil Psalm 110:1.

options.text: He remained on earth to lead the disciples in person.
options.note: He ascended to heaven and was seated at God's right hand.

options.text: He ascended to heaven to reign at God's right hand.
options.note: Correct, but it should note that from there He worked with the disciples, confirming His word.

options.text: He disappeared without any further word.
options.note: He ascended visibly after commissioning them, and continued to work through them.

questions: The women came expecting to anoint a dead body and found a risen Lord. Where might God be about to surprise your low expectations with resurrection hope?
The risen Jesus sent word specifically to Peter, who had denied Him. How does His grace to failures like Peter speak to your own failures?
Jesus rebuked the disciples' unbelief in the resurrection reports. Where are you slow to believe what God has clearly promised?
The risen Lord sends His people to preach the gospel to every creature. What part might God be calling you to play in that mission where you are?
Jesus reigns now at God's right hand and works with His witnesses. How does knowing the crucified Jesus is the reigning Lord strengthen you to live and speak for Him?

translationNotes.note: Many of the earliest and most reliable manuscripts end the Gospel here, with the women fleeing in fear. This abrupt ending throws the reader back on Jesus' own promise that He is risen and will be seen in Galilee, and calls for a response of faith.

These verses, the longer ending, are absent from the earliest manuscripts and differ in style from the rest of Mark; some manuscripts have a shorter ending instead. They are printed here as part of the received text, and their content, the appearances, the commission, and the ascension, agrees with the other Gospels and Acts, but distinctive doctrines or practices should not be built on them alone.

The accompanying signs describe the confirming works of the apostolic era; they are not a promise that every believer will handle snakes or drink poison safely, and they must never be turned into a test of faith. Read them in light of the clearer teaching of the whole New Testament.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 16:1-20. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. The longer ending, verses 9 to 20, is included as printed in the BSB, with the manuscript question noted honestly in translationNotes. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
